subject: Physics
prerequisites: Vectors and scalars; adding vectors and resolving into perpendicular components
Rearranging equations and working with SI units and significant figures
Interpreting the gradient and area of a graph

learning_objectives.statement: Use the equations of uniformly accelerated motion (the SUVAT equations) for straight-line motion, including vertical motion under gravity with g = 9.81 m/s^2.

Interpret displacement-time and velocity-time graphs: the gradient of a displacement-time graph gives velocity, the gradient of a velocity-time graph gives acceleration, and the area under a velocity-time graph gives displacement.
Resolve a force into two perpendicular components, find the resultant of coplanar forces, and apply the conditions for an object to be in equilibrium.
Apply Newton's three laws of motion, using resultant force F = ma, to single objects and to connected systems such as masses linked by a light inextensible string over a pulley.
Explain, in terms of the balance between driving force and drag, why an object moving through a fluid reaches a terminal (maximum) velocity.

depth_profile: Edexcel A-Level Physics (9PH0) rigour. Students work confidently with the SUVAT equations (selecting the correct equation and handling vertical motion under gravity at g = 9.81 m/s^2), interpret motion graphs quantitatively (gradient and area, including tangents to curves), resolve forces into perpendicular components and analyse two-dimensional equilibrium, apply Newton's laws to connected systems, and reason about terminal velocity through force balance. Expect multi-step problems, algebraic 'show that' derivations, correct units and significant figures, and clear force diagrams. Momentum and impulse sit in the same topic on the full specification but are NOT part of this set - keep to kinematics, force resolution, Newton's laws, and drag/terminal velocity.
assessment_notes: Edexcel A-Level Physics is examined through structured questions of increasing length, including 'show that' derivations, extended calculations, and short explanations, with links to the required practicals (e.g. investigating motion or measuring g). Marks are method-based: a correct free-body/force diagram, resolving forces correctly, the correct equation with substitution, and correct units and significant figures each earn marks. A 'show that' question requires every step to be shown and usually a final value quoted to one more figure than the target. The value g = 9.81 m/s^2 is provided in the Data, Formulae and Relationships Booklet; wrong or missing units are penalised.
reference_data: Use these values consistently in EVERY worked example and practice question; do not substitute other figures: acceleration of free fall / gravitational field strength g = 9.81 m/s^2 (9.81 N/kg). VALIDATE against the Edexcel A-Level Physics (9PH0) Data, Formulae and Relationships Booklet before classroom use; this table only guarantees all sections use the SAME value of g.
source: Hand-seeded for POC - validate against the Edexcel A-Level Physics specification (9PH0), Topic 3 (Motion and forces).

exam_map.value: Papers 1–3; structured questions incl. **'show that'** derivations and required-practical links
SUVAT, motion graphs, **resolving forces**, connected systems, and terminal velocity
Pick the right SUVAT equation; resolve forces along and perpendicular to the motion
Correct **units** and a clear force diagram earn method marks even if the final answer slips

spec_source_citation: Edexcel A-Level Physics (9PH0) specification, Topic 3 — Motion and forces (validate against the official spec).

spec_checklist.can_do: I can use the equations of uniformly accelerated motion (the SUVAT equations) for straight-line motion, including vertical motion under gravity with g = 9.81 m/s^2.

I can interpret displacement-time and velocity-time graphs: the gradient of a displacement-time graph gives velocity, the gradient of a velocity-time graph gives acceleration, and the area under a velocity-time graph gives displacement.
I can resolve a force into two perpendicular components, find the resultant of coplanar forces, and apply the conditions for an object to be in equilibrium.
I can apply Newton's three laws of motion, using resultant force F = ma, to single objects and to connected systems such as masses linked by a light inextensible string over a pulley.
I can explain, in terms of the balance between driving force and drag, why an object moving through a fluid reaches a terminal (maximum) velocity.
